findings.title: BVI FSC full enforcement of beneficial ownership regime from April 1, 2026; escalating penalties and strike-off risk
findings.summary: The BVI FSC entered full enforcement mode on April 1, 2026. Entities that missed the January 1, 2026 beneficial ownership filing deadline are accruing penalties of $600 per entity for the first three months (April–June 2026), rising to $800 for the following three months (July–September 2026), with strike-off possible after six months of non-compliance (approximately October 2026). Legitimate-interest access to the register opened simultaneously on April 1, 2026.
findings.client_impact: Clients holding BVI entities that have not yet filed beneficial ownership information are already incurring monetary penalties and face potential strike-off of their vehicle—and loss of the underlying structure or assets—from approximately October 2026. Immediate filing with their registered agent is required to stop the penalty clock.
findings.urgency_reason: The filing deadline passed January 1, 2026; penalties have been running since April 1, 2026. The penalty tier escalates to $800 per entity in approximately five weeks (July 1), and strike-off risk materialises in approximately four months. Clients who have not yet filed must act now.

findings.title: UAE FATF Mutual Evaluation begins June 2026; AML inspections intensifying now under tightened Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025
findings.summary: The UAE Central Bank released updated AML guidance on April 16, 2026, transitioning regulated institutions to a risk-based, data-driven compliance framework ahead of the FATF Mutual Evaluation commencing June 2026. Inspections are intensifying in frequency and depth right now. Under Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025, 'should have known' liability can attach even where an institution had no actual knowledge that funds were illicit.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients holding assets or operating through UAE-regulated entities (banks, wealth managers, fund managers) face intensified due diligence requests—including updated KYC documentation, source-of-funds evidence, and beneficial ownership confirmations—as inspectors assess whether AML controls work in practice. Delays or gaps in documentation increase the risk of account restrictions or regulatory censure of the client's service providers.
findings.urgency_reason: The FATF Mutual Evaluation begins in June 2026, within approximately 30 days. UAE regulated institutions are already conducting heightened reviews. Clients with UAE-regulated accounts or structures should proactively refresh their documentation and confirm compliance with local advisors immediately.

findings.title: DAC 9 / GloBE Information Return: June 30, 2026 filing deadline approaching in Luxembourg and across EU jurisdictions
findings.summary: EU Directive 2025/872 (DAC 9), transposed by Luxembourg and other EU member states by end-2025, requires in-scope MNEs (consolidated annual revenues ≥ €750 million) to file the GloBE Top-Up Tax Information Return (GIR) by June 30, 2026 in many jurisdictions. The OECD's May 18, 2026 guidance formally addressed late-filing penalties and technical challenges with the Multilateral Competent Authority Agreement.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with ownership interests in MNE groups at or above the €750 million revenue threshold using Luxembourg holding or financing vehicles must confirm that their GIR has been prepared and is ready to file. Clients below the Luxembourg threshold should also verify whether consolidated affiliates trigger the obligation in other EU member states.
findings.urgency_reason: The GIR filing deadline is June 30, 2026 — 36 days from today — and preparation of this technically complex return cannot realistically begin after June 24. Clients and their tax advisors must confirm filing readiness immediately.

findings.title: OECD May 18, 2026 Pillar Two guidance package: late-filing penalty framework formalised, UTPR safe harbour updated, GIR Central Record revised
findings.summary: On May 18, 2026, the OECD released coordinated Pillar Two updates: (1) a common understanding on late-filing penalties for the first GIR cycle; (2) further Administrative Guidance on the Transitional UTPR Safe Harbour for 52-53-week fiscal years; and (3) an updated Central Record of qualifying status jurisdictions. The June 30, 2026 GIR deadline is now the subject of an active, quantified penalty framework.
findings.client_impact: Clients with interests in MNE groups subject to Pillar Two must confirm their group's GIR compliance calendar with their tax advisors: the June 30, 2026 first-filing deadline now carries formalised late penalties. The UTPR safe harbour update may also affect the effective tax position of non-resident entities within the group structure.
findings.urgency_reason: The June 30, 2026 GIR deadline is 36 days away. Late-filing penalties are now formalised by the May 18 OECD package. Immediate action by clients' tax advisors is required.

findings.title: BVI beneficial ownership register: 'obliged entities' and legitimate-interest access opened April 2026
findings.summary: Beneficial ownership data in the BVI statutory register is now accessible from April 2026 to 'obliged entities' (such as registered agents and financial institutions) and to individuals demonstrating 'legitimate interest' (25%+ direct or indirect ownership or equivalent control). The register is not public; law enforcement access remains unrestricted. Approved exemption applications protect commercially sensitive information from legitimate-interest disclosure.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients using BVI entities should be aware that their ownership information — if filed — is now visible to counterparty banks, registered agents, and others who can establish legitimate interest. Clients with commercially or personally sensitive ownership arrangements should discuss exemption applications with their BVI counsel before their ownership data is accessed.
findings.urgency_reason: Access opened April 1, 2026 and is already in effect. No immediate deadline, but clients should assess exposure and decide whether to apply for exemptions proactively.

findings.title: BCB Resolution No. 561 bans crypto and stablecoin settlement in eFX cross-border payments effective October 1, 2026
findings.summary: BCB Resolution No. 561 (published April 30, 2026) prohibits eFX providers from using stablecoins or other virtual assets — including Bitcoin — to settle overseas remittances, effective October 1, 2026. Stablecoins account for roughly 90% of crypto-linked international transfers from Brazil. All eFX payments must henceforth settle via traditional FX transactions or non-resident real accounts.
findings.client_impact: Brazilian HNW clients who currently use stablecoin or crypto-denominated channels to transfer wealth internationally must migrate to traditional FX mechanisms before October 1, 2026, or risk failed and blocked transactions. This will likely increase costs and settlement times for cross-border capital movements from Brazil.
findings.urgency_reason: The effective date is October 1, 2026 — approximately four months away. Clients relying on crypto channels for cross-border remittances from Brazil should begin transitioning to compliant FX mechanisms now to avoid operational disruption at the deadline.

findings.title: BCB VASP authorization regime in force from February 2, 2026; existing operators must apply by October 30, 2026
findings.summary: The BCB's VASP regulatory framework (Resolutions No. 519, 520, and 521) entered into force February 2, 2026. Existing VASP operators must formally apply for BCB authorization by October 30, 2026 or cease operations. The framework subjects VASPs to AML/KYC obligations, capital requirements, and CVM/BCB oversight.
findings.client_impact: Clients who own, operate, or hold material investments in Brazilian VASP or crypto exchange businesses face a hard regulatory authorization deadline. Unlicensed operation after October 30, 2026 is impermissible; the BCB application process requires months of preparation, making early filing essential.
findings.urgency_reason: The October 30, 2026 deadline is approximately five months away, but the BCB authorization process requires extensive documentation and compliance infrastructure build-out. Clients with VASP interests in Brazil should begin the authorization process now.

findings.title: BCB enhanced foreign exchange transaction reporting obligations in force from May 2026
findings.summary: From May 2026, market participants must provide additional information to Brazil's Central Bank regarding foreign exchange market transactions and foreign capital operations in Brazil, aligning with the BCB's broader effort to strengthen oversight of cross-border capital flows and reduce regulatory blind spots, particularly related to crypto-facilitated transfers.
findings.client_impact: HNW Brazilian clients making cross-border transfers — whether through banks, brokerage accounts, or capital market operations — should expect increased reporting and documentation requirements from their Brazilian financial institutions, potentially including enhanced source-of-funds disclosures.
findings.urgency_reason: The enhanced reporting requirement is already in force as of May 2026. Clients and their local advisors should confirm that existing cross-border transaction documentation meets the new BCB standards to avoid compliance friction.

findings.title: FinCEN Interim Final Rule: all US-formed entities exempt from BOI reporting; foreign-formed US-registered entities retain the obligation
findings.summary: FinCEN's Interim Final Rule (published March 26, 2025, with final rulemaking ongoing in 2026) exempts all entities created in the United States from CTA beneficial ownership information reporting. Only entities formed under foreign law and registered to do business in a US state or tribal jurisdiction remain 'reporting companies.' Foreign reporting companies are also no longer required to list US persons as beneficial owners.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients who hold foreign-formed entities (e.g., BVI, Cayman, Panama companies) registered to do business in US states remain subject to FinCEN BOI reporting and must ensure filings are current. By contrast, US LLCs and corporations held by these clients are now fully exempt, simplifying the US compliance footprint for dual-layer holding structures.
findings.urgency_reason: The Interim Final Rule is in effect; FinCEN is finalising the permanent rule in 2026. Foreign entities registered in the US should verify their current reporting status and confirm compliance as the final rule could impose revised deadlines upon enactment.

findings.title: One Big Beautiful Bill Act: federal estate and gift tax exemption permanently raised to $15M per individual ($30M per couple) effective January 1, 2026
findings.summary: Signed July 4, 2025, the OBBBA permanently raised the federal estate and gift tax basic exclusion amount to $15 million per individual ($30 million for married couples), indexed annually for inflation, effective January 1, 2026. The anticipated TCJA sunset did not occur. The 2026 annual gift tax exclusion is $19,000 per donee. Eighteen US states and jurisdictions retain independent estate or inheritance taxes at thresholds below the new federal exemption.
findings.client_impact: HNW US clients should review their estate plans: irrevocable SLATs, GRATs, IDGTs, and other structures engineered around the anticipated 2025 TCJA sunset may now be over-engineered or require amendment. The doubled exemption creates significant lifetime gifting opportunities — including gifts to trusts or family members — while residual state estate tax exposure in the 18 remaining state-tax jurisdictions must be re-evaluated.
findings.urgency_reason: The change is in effect from January 1, 2026. No hard deadline, but clients with legacy estate plans designed around the lower pre-TCJA exemption or the anticipated sunset should update their structures to take full advantage of the current exclusion amount and address any over-hedging in existing irrevocable vehicles.

findings.title: Florida SB 264 foreign buyer restrictions upheld in November 2025; near-total ban on Chinese nationals and proximity restrictions remain fully in force
findings.summary: Florida SB 264 (effective July 1, 2023) was upheld by courts in November 2025 and remains in full force in 2026. Chinese nationals face a near-total ban on purchasing any Florida real estate. Nationals from six other 'countries of concern' (Russia, Iran, North Korea, Cuba, Venezuela, Syria) are restricted from acquiring property within 20 miles of military installations or critical infrastructure. All buyers must sign compliance affidavits; violations can result in property forfeiture and criminal charges.
findings.client_impact: Chinese national HNW clients must not purchase any Florida real estate without specialist legal counsel to assess whether a narrow exemption applies. Clients from the six other listed countries must verify proximity to restricted zones before any Florida acquisition. All affected foreign national buyers must complete and retain compliance affidavits for every Florida transaction.
findings.urgency_reason: The law is in effect and legally confirmed. No active deadline, but the consequences of non-compliance — forfeiture and criminal charges — make this a standing compliance requirement for every Florida real estate transaction involving affected nationalities.

findings.title: Cayman Islands tokenised fund regulatory framework in force from March 24, 2026
findings.summary: Three Amendment Acts commenced March 24, 2026, extending CIMA's existing fund regulation to tokenised mutual and private funds. Tokenised funds must meet all standard regulatory requirements plus additional obligations specific to digital equity or investment tokens. CIMA has expanded supervisory powers to inspect the technology infrastructure supporting tokenisation and digital token transactions.
findings.client_impact: Clients with interests in Cayman-domiciled tokenised investment funds — or those considering launching tokenised fund structures — should confirm that offering documents, CIMA registration, and operational processes are updated to reflect the new framework. Unregistered tokenised funds operating after March 24 are out of compliance.
findings.urgency_reason: The framework has been in force since March 24, 2026. Clients and fund managers with existing tokenised fund structures should confirm compliance with Cayman counsel immediately; new structures must be designed under the new rules from inception.

findings.title: MAS tightens 13O/13U family office AUM conditions: minimum AUM must be maintained throughout the full fund life from May 2026
findings.summary: From May 2026, MAS requires the applicable minimum AUM threshold under the Section 13O and 13U single family office tax exemption schemes to be maintained throughout the entire life of the fund — not merely at inception or year-end. Structures that drop below the threshold mid-year risk losing tax-exempt status retroactively for that year. The schemes remain available until December 31, 2029 under progressively tighter qualifying conditions.
findings.client_impact: Singapore family office clients must implement continuous AUM monitoring and establish internal triggers for corrective action (e.g., capital calls, asset transfers) before breaching the threshold mid-year. A temporary dip — previously manageable with year-end rebalancing — can now result in loss of tax exemption for the entire fiscal year.
findings.urgency_reason: The tightened condition is in effect from May 2026. Family offices should review their AUM management practices and governance frameworks now to ensure continuous compliance going forward.

findings.title: MAS proposed SFO licensing framework: class exemption from SFA licensing with mandatory notification and annual reporting, anticipated in 2026
findings.summary: MAS is expected to implement its Single Family Office licensing framework in 2026, following the November 2024 Response to Feedback. The framework introduces a class exemption from Securities and Futures Act licensing for qualifying SFOs but imposes mandatory MAS notification and annual reporting. Existing SFOs will have a six-month transition period once the framework is finalised.
findings.client_impact: Singapore single family office clients that currently operate outside the licensing framework should begin preparing MAS notification submissions and ensuring their governance, reporting, and structure documentation meet the incoming requirements, so they can file promptly within the six-month window once the final framework is published.
findings.urgency_reason: Implementation is anticipated in 2026. The six-month transition clock starts only upon publication of the final framework, but given the notification and reporting infrastructure required, advance preparation now avoids a last-minute compliance rush.

findings.title: DFSA AML and Glossary Module amendments in force March 2, 2026: tighter 'should have known' liability standard for DIFC-regulated entities
findings.summary: The DFSA's amended AML Module and updated Glossary came into force on March 2, 2026, aligning DIFC AML requirements with UAE Federal Decree-Law No. 10 of 2025. The amendments introduce a tighter 'should have known' liability standard for regulated entities and were accompanied by updated DFSA FAQs.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients operating through DIFC-regulated entities — such as family office platforms, fund managers, or investment advisors — should confirm that their DIFC-regulated service providers have updated their AML procedures to reflect the new module, and that client due diligence files meet the heightened 'should have known' standard.
findings.urgency_reason: The amendments are already in force. Regulated entities in the DIFC that have not updated their AML frameworks since March 2, 2026 are in breach of current rules; clients should verify their DIFC-based advisors' compliance posture.

findings.title: UAE Capital Markets Authority replaces SCA from January 1, 2026; onshore wealth managers must verify licence migration
findings.summary: The UAE Capital Markets Authority (CMA) succeeded the Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) on January 1, 2026 and now regulates all securities-related activity in onshore UAE (outside DIFC and ADGM), including fund management, asset management, and fund distribution. Wealth managers and fund managers must verify whether SCA registrations and licences have been migrated to the CMA.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with onshore UAE (non-DIFC, non-ADGM) wealth management mandates or fund investments should confirm with their managers that registrations have been properly migrated to the CMA and that all licences, product approvals, and client agreements are valid under the new authority.
findings.urgency_reason: The transition occurred January 1, 2026 and is in effect. Unconfirmed migration may create gaps in regulatory standing for onshore UAE-regulated managers; clients should request written confirmation of CMA authorisation.

findings.title: DIFC Variable Capital Company Regulations enacted February 9, 2026: new flexible sub-fund vehicle for private wealth structuring
findings.summary: The DIFC Authority enacted the Variable Capital Company (VCC) Regulations on February 9, 2026, introducing a new flexible capital vehicle for investment funds and wealth management structures in the DIFC. The VCC can house multiple sub-funds with segregated assets and liabilities, functioning similarly to Cayman SPC or Singapore VCC structures.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients structuring family office vehicles, private funds, or multi-strategy investments in the DIFC now have access to a sub-fund-segregated vehicle that enables multi-asset or multi-beneficiary wealth structures to be consolidated into a single regulated UAE entity. This is directly relevant for clients considering a DIFC wealth structuring hub.
findings.urgency_reason: The VCC is available now as a new structuring option. No deadline or compliance obligation attaches to existing structures, but clients planning new DIFC fund or wealth structures should evaluate the VCC alongside Cayman and Singapore alternatives.

findings.title: Swiss FTA CRS 2.0 guidance in effect: tie-breaker rule eliminated for multiple-residence self-certifications
findings.summary: In early 2026, the Swiss Federal Tax Administration issued updated guidance aligning Switzerland's domestic rules with OECD CRS 2.0. The revised standard eliminates the ability of individuals with multiple tax residencies to use tax-treaty tie-breaker rules to self-certify a single jurisdiction. Swiss financial institutions must now collect and report for all relevant residency jurisdictions on updated self-certification forms.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Swiss bank or investment accounts who hold multiple tax residencies — for example, a client with ongoing ties to a home country while resident in the UAE or Singapore — must update their CRS self-certifications at Swiss institutions to reflect all residency jurisdictions, or risk the institution filing incomplete CRS reports that trigger follow-up from multiple tax authorities.
findings.urgency_reason: The guidance is in force and Swiss institutions are actively updating onboarding and KYC processes. Clients with multiple residencies holding Swiss accounts should expect self-certification update requests and should respond promptly to avoid account restrictions.

findings.title: Cyprus comprehensive tax reform in force January 1, 2026: 15% corporate tax, 5% SDC on dividends, 8% crypto flat tax, non-dom extension option
findings.summary: Cyprus enacted sweeping tax reform effective January 1, 2026, raising corporate income tax from 12.5% to 15% (aligned with OECD Pillar Two minimum); reducing the Special Defence Contribution on actual dividends from 17% to 5%; abolishing stamp duty; introducing an 8% flat tax on crypto disposals (Article 20E); raising the personal income tax-free threshold to €22,000; and offering existing non-domiciled residents the option to extend their SDC exemption by five additional years via a €250,000 lump-sum payment.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients using Cyprus holding or operating companies should model the combined impact of the corporate rate increase and the significant SDC reduction on dividend distributions. Non-dom residents approaching the end of their 17-year SDC exemption period should evaluate whether the lump-sum extension is cost-effective, and clients with crypto assets held through Cyprus structures must account for the new 8% disposal tax when planning realisations.
findings.urgency_reason: All changes are effective from January 1, 2026 and currently in force. Tax advisors should review Cyprus structures for the combined impact across corporate, dividend, and crypto tax dimensions in any 2026 distribution or restructuring planning.

findings.title: Bahamas implementing periodic beneficial ownership validation and expanded FATF Recommendation 25 trustee obligations in 2026
findings.summary: In 2026, the Bahamas is implementing periodic validation requirements for beneficial ownership information under its post-FATF-greylisting reform framework. Legislative amendments now require trustees and equivalent position holders to obtain and maintain beneficial ownership information per FATF Recommendation 25. Ongoing regulatory pressure aims to maintain the Bahamas' off-grey-list standing (removed in 2022).
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Bahamas-domiciled trusts, companies, or funds should confirm with their trustees and registered agents that beneficial ownership records are current and meet the periodic validation requirements, to avoid compliance gaps that could trigger enforcement or heightened scrutiny under the strengthened AML framework.
findings.urgency_reason: Requirements are in force in 2026. No specific single deadline, but periodic validation means records not updated since the 2022 grey-list removal may now be considered stale and trigger enforcement inquiries.

findings.title: Trusts (Jersey) 8th Amendment in force March 20, 2026: beneficiary termination rights restricted, secured lender priority clarified, sole trustee resignation blocked
findings.summary: The eighth amendment to the Trusts (Jersey) Law 1984 commenced March 20, 2026, introducing three key changes: (1) beneficiaries cannot compel termination of a trust if a power to add beneficiaries or charitable purpose provisions exist; (2) secured lenders have statutory priority over trustee equitable liens on trust assets unless otherwise agreed; (3) a sole trustee cannot resign without a successor in place.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Jersey trusts that include secured lending arrangements — such as trustee-backed loans or trust-financed real estate — should review whether the new lender priority provisions affect existing credit agreements. Trust deeds with broad add-beneficiary powers should also be reviewed, as beneficiaries now have materially restricted rights to compel termination.
findings.urgency_reason: The amendment is in force from March 20, 2026. Clients with existing Jersey trusts — particularly those with financing arrangements — should ask their trustees and Jersey counsel to review trust deeds and any lending agreements in light of the new provisions.

findings.title: JFSC AML/CFT scope expanded to cover Jersey trustees; FATF-aligned trustee CDD obligations now in effect
findings.summary: Recent amendments to Jersey's AML/CFT legislation have expanded regulatory scope to cover Jersey entities acting as trustees, marking one of the most significant recent changes to the jurisdiction's financial crime regime. Trustees are now subject to full client due diligence and beneficial ownership verification requirements aligned with FATF Recommendations. The JFSC published a Basel III implementation update on May 19, 2026.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Jersey-regulated trustees should expect enhanced due diligence requests — including updated KYC documentation, source-of-funds evidence for trust assets, and beneficial ownership verification for settlors, beneficiaries, and protectors — as Jersey trustees bring their AML/CFT procedures into compliance with the expanded scope.
findings.urgency_reason: The trustee AML/CFT expansion is in force and Jersey trustees are actively updating compliance frameworks. Clients who have not recently updated KYC files with their Jersey trustees should do so proactively to avoid administrative holds.

findings.title: CARF and CRS 2.0 data collection in force from January 1, 2026 across 67 jurisdictions; first reporting exchange expected 2027
findings.summary: From January 1, 2026, crypto-asset service providers in 67 committed jurisdictions — including all EU member states under DAC8 — must collect data on reportable users and transactions under CARF and updated CRS 2.0 standards. First information exchange reporting is expected in 2027. The US is not expected to participate until at least 2028. Switzerland's CRS 2.0 guidance is also in effect.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients holding crypto assets through exchanges, custodians, or platforms in any of the 67 participating jurisdictions should assume their ownership and transaction data is being collected now and will be reported to their tax residence jurisdictions in 2027. Clients with undisclosed crypto holdings or cross-border crypto arrangements should seek immediate voluntary disclosure advice — the collection window that precedes first exchange is closing.
findings.urgency_reason: Data collection is already underway as of January 1, 2026. While the first exchange is in 2027, the underlying data cannot be retroactively excluded once collected. Clients needing to regularise their position should act before the 2027 exchange date.

findings.title: Luxembourg Draft Law 8676 proposes elimination of joint income tax filing from January 1, 2028
findings.summary: Introduced January 6, 2026, Draft Law 8676 proposes replacing Luxembourg's joint tax filing system with a single personal income tax class and full individual taxation, effective January 1, 2028, with a 25-year transitional period for currently jointly-filed couples. This is a significant structural change with direct implications for HNWI and family wealth planning structures that rely on joint filing benefits.
findings.client_impact: HNW families resident in Luxembourg who rely on joint filing to optimise personal income tax should model the impact of individual taxation from 2028 and consider whether compensation arrangements, investment income flows, and family wealth structures should be restructured before the effective date.
findings.urgency_reason: The draft law is proposed with a 2028 effective date. The 25-year transition reflects legislative seriousness; clients should begin scenario modelling and planning discussions with Luxembourg tax advisors during 2026 to allow adequate time for restructuring.

findings.title: FIRPTA withholding rates unchanged at 15%; IRS moving toward mandatory electronic filing; blocker corporation regulations still pending
findings.summary: FIRPTA withholding rates remain at 15% of amount realised (10% for residences sold at $1 million or below; exemption for primary residences at $300,000 or below). The IRS is moving to mandate EFTPS electronic payment for FIRPTA withholding. Proposed regulations targeting blocker corporations used by foreign investors in US real property (REG-100442-22) remain under consideration with no final rule as of May 2026.
findings.client_impact: Foreign national HNW clients selling US real estate continue to be subject to the established FIRPTA withholding rates. The pending blocker corporation regulations, if finalised, would limit a common tax-efficient structuring technique for foreign investment in US real property — clients with existing blocker structures should monitor this rulemaking.
findings.urgency_reason: No changes have occurred and the pending regulations have no final effective date. This is background awareness for clients with US real estate holdings.

findings.title: CIMA annual fund fees increased effective January 1, 2026
findings.summary: CIMA raised annual fees for regulated investment funds effective January 1, 2026: registered fund Annual Return Fees from $3,675 to $4,125; Master Fund fees from $2,625 to $3,075; and sub-fund fees for registered mutual funds from $300 to $750 per sub-fund.
findings.client_impact: Clients holding Cayman-domiciled investment funds will see modestly higher annual CIMA fees reflected in their fund's operating expenses. No structural or compliance action is required beyond updating fee provisions in fund documentation if applicable.
findings.urgency_reason: Operational cost update already in effect since January 1, 2026. No action required beyond budget adjustments.

findings.title: Cayman Islands NRA 2025-2026 Factsheet updated May 2026 ahead of CFATF mutual evaluation in 2027
findings.summary: The Cayman Islands Government updated its 2025-2026 National Risk Assessment Factsheet in May 2026, including an updated Inherent Risk Assessment delayed approximately five months. The NRA is a preparatory step for the fifth-round CFATF mutual evaluation scheduled to commence in 2027.
findings.client_impact: Cayman-based fund and entity structures are unlikely to face immediate regulatory changes from the NRA update, but the approaching CFATF evaluation in 2027 signals continued regulatory intensity. Clients and their fund managers should expect enhanced KYC requests in the lead-up to and during the evaluation.
findings.urgency_reason: Background awareness. The CFATF evaluation begins in 2027; no immediate client action required.

findings.title: Luxembourg 2026 tax package: new 20% startup investment credit, pension deduction ceiling raised, social security increases
findings.summary: Effective January 1, 2026: a new 20% tax credit (capped at €100,000/year) for qualifying investments in young innovative companies; the private pension deduction ceiling raised from €3,200 to €4,500/year; pension social security rates increased from 24% to 25.5% until 2032; and a new €9,000/year 'extended working life' allowance for individuals continuing past early retirement eligibility.
findings.client_impact: Luxembourg-based HNW clients should discuss with their tax advisors whether qualifying startup investments generate the new 20% credit and whether optimising private pension contributions up to the increased €4,500 deduction ceiling makes sense, balanced against the higher social security contributions.
findings.urgency_reason: All changes are in effect from January 1, 2026. These are planning considerations for the 2026 tax year with no further deadline.

findings.title: OECD releases Pillar Two GMT implementation toolkit April 30, 2026
findings.summary: The OECD published a practical implementation toolkit for the Global Minimum Tax on April 30, 2026, providing a roadmap for jurisdictions and MNEs implementing Pillar Two GloBE rules. The toolkit accompanies the February 2026 'Side-by-Side Package' and new safe harbour guidance, and is timed to the approaching June 30, 2026 GIR filing deadline.
findings.client_impact: Clients with interests in MNE groups subject to Pillar Two can direct their tax advisors to the OECD's toolkit for practical GloBE compliance guidance. No new obligation is created by the toolkit itself — actionable GIR filing items are covered in the separate DAC 9 and OECD May 18 guidance findings above.
findings.urgency_reason: Informational resource from the OECD. No new deadline created by this publication.

findings.title: Switzerland delays FATCA Model 1 IGA transition to January 1, 2028
findings.summary: Switzerland has deferred its transition from a Model 2 (individual consent-based) FATCA IGA to a reciprocal Model 1 IGA with the United States until January 1, 2028, as confirmed by a February 2026 KPMG advisory. The Model 1 IGA will ultimately provide automatic and reciprocal information exchange between the Swiss FTA and the IRS.
findings.client_impact: US persons with Swiss accounts benefit from the continued Model 2 framework until 2028, maintaining slightly more control over information flow in the near term. However, clients and their Swiss financial institutions should begin preparing for the enhanced automatic Model 1 reporting obligations well ahead of the 2028 effective date.
findings.urgency_reason: The transition has been delayed to 2028 — positive news for US clients with Swiss accounts. No immediate action required.

findings.title: Panama UBO registry compliance continuing under Law 129 of 2020; FATF confirmation of strengthened controls
findings.summary: Panama's UBO registry under Law 129 of 2020 requires all companies to register ultimate beneficial owners with their resident agent in a confidential government database (not public), with no minimum ownership threshold. The FATF confirmed Panama strengthened its beneficial ownership verification and FIU capabilities, supporting its removal from the FATF grey list.
findings.client_impact: Clients using Panamanian entities should confirm with their resident agent that all UBO registrations are current and that KYC documentation — certified passports, bank references, proof of address for all directors, shareholders, and UBOs — is up to date. Panama's strengthened FATF standing makes it a more defensible offshore jurisdiction choice.
findings.urgency_reason: Ongoing compliance requirement under established law. No new deadline or change; background awareness for clients with Panama structures.

findings.title: Malta non-dom regime confirmed for 2026: no inheritance, estate, wealth, or gift tax; 15% flat rate on remitted foreign income
findings.summary: Malta's Non-Domiciled tax regime remains in force in 2026 with no inheritance tax, no estate tax, no wealth tax, and no gift tax. Foreign investment income is taxable only if remitted to Malta, subject to a minimum €5,000 annual charge and a 15% flat rate under the Resident Non-Dom scheme. Capital gains on securities remain exempt.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients considering a change of domicile or an alternative European base for family wealth management should note Malta's zero-inheritance, zero-estate-tax environment and comparatively low flat rate on remitted income. Comprehensive planning — including domicile analysis and remittance strategy — is required before relying on the regime.
findings.urgency_reason: No change to the Malta non-dom regime; this is a status confirmation for 2026. Background awareness for clients evaluating European relocation or succession planning scenarios.

findings.title: GFSC AML/CFT/CPF Handbook updated May 8, 2026 following strong MONEYVAL outcome
findings.summary: The Guernsey Financial Services Commission updated its Handbook on Countering Financial Crime on May 8, 2026, embedding heightened governance and AML/CFT expectations for all regulated firms in Guernsey's financial services sector, following a strong MONEYVAL mutual evaluation outcome.
findings.client_impact: HNW clients with Guernsey-domiciled structures — funds, trusts, or holding companies — should expect their Guernsey-regulated service providers to apply the updated Handbook requirements in their next scheduled CDD review cycle, including enhanced governance documentation and AML risk assessment updates.
findings.urgency_reason: Routine compliance update to an established handbook. No immediate client action required, though clients should remain available to respond to CDD requests from Guernsey-regulated entities as they update their procedures.
